entity_catalog.name: Mohammadali Jannatkhahdoost
entity_catalog.role: Author of the book; Iranian thinker; self-described as independent theorist

entity_catalog.name: Taha Doostmohammadi
entity_catalog.role: Translator of the book into English

entity_catalog.name: Kurt Gödel
entity_catalog.role: Austrian-American logician and mathematician; author of Incompleteness Theorems; cited as foundational for the theory's formal-system framework

entity_catalog.name: Friedrich Hayek
entity_catalog.role: Austrian-British economist; cited for free banking, spontaneous order, and critique of central planning

entity_catalog.name: Murray Rothbard
entity_catalog.role: American libertarian economist; cited approvingly for property rights but critiqued for four theoretical gaps

entity_catalog.name: Ludwig von Mises
entity_catalog.role: Austrian economist; praxeology and Austrian School; cited approvingly

entity_catalog.name: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel
entity_catalog.role: German philosopher; cited as source of dialectical mysticism, collectivism, and statism; traced genealogy to Nazism and communism

entity_catalog.name: Karl Marx
entity_catalog.role: German philosopher/economist; cited as recycling Hegelian mysticism into socialist state-worship

entity_catalog.name: Thomas Hobbes
entity_catalog.role: English philosopher; author of Leviathan; cited for monopoly-of-violence theory; described as 'more honest than Locke' but still inconsistent

entity_catalog.name: John Locke
entity_catalog.role: English philosopher; cited for property rights theory but deemed less consistent than Hobbes

entity_catalog.name: Bertrand Russell
entity_catalog.role: British philosopher/mathematician; critiqued for reducing religion to a reaction to fear

entity_catalog.name: Yuval Noah Harari
entity_catalog.role: Israeli historian; cited as inverting the role of religion in civilization; initial intellectual catalyst for the theory

entity_catalog.name: Clayton Christensen
entity_catalog.role: American business theorist; cited for religion's role as moral foundation of the market

entity_catalog.name: Daron Acemoglu
entity_catalog.role: Economist; author of 'The Narrow Corridor'; critiqued as offering another deception against liberty through balance-of-power framework

entity_catalog.name: Leonard Peikoff
entity_catalog.role: Objectivist philosopher; cited for genealogy from Plato and Kant to Hegel to Auschwitz

entity_catalog.name: Ayaan Hirsi Ali
entity_catalog.role: Somali-Dutch activist; cited as representative of New Atheism's inability to succeed in a civilizational war

entity_catalog.name: Ali Shariati
entity_catalog.role: Iranian sociologist/revolutionary thinker; cited as part of the lineage of Iranian-Islamic mysticism and the silencing of reason

entity_catalog.name: Ronald Reagan
entity_catalog.role: American president; cited as spark for the theory of liberty; symbolic of market freedom

entity_catalog.name: Prophet Muhammad (PBUH)
entity_catalog.role: Founder of Islam; his governance cited as lacking a modern state structure; Ghadir Khumm event attributed to him

entity_catalog.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib (AS)
entity_catalog.role: First Imam of Shi'ism; his caliphate analyzed as a struggle for liberty and property rights; Ghadir Khumm as wilayah transfer

entity_catalog.name: Imam Husayn (AS)
entity_catalog.role: Third Imam; his uprising analyzed as resistance to tyranny, not a quest for power

entity_catalog.name: Imam Musa al-Kazim (AS)
entity_catalog.role: Seventh Imam; cited for 'two proofs' doctrine (outward: Imams; inward: reason); his life as model of resistance without surrender

entity_catalog.name: Imam Mahdi (AS)
entity_catalog.role: Twelfth Imam; his occultation and expected advent cited as essential to completing the theory of liberty; Mahdism as logical necessity

entity_catalog.name: Mu'awiyah
entity_catalog.role: Umayyad caliph; cited for confiscating religion in favor of the state; analogized to Abu Sufyan's pattern

entity_catalog.name: 'Umar ibn al-Khattab
entity_catalog.role: Second caliph; cited in context of taxation and historical distortion of Ahl al-Bayt

entity_catalog.name: Arash the Archer
entity_catalog.role: Mythological Iranian hero; metaphor used in prefatory letter for the book's civilizational reach

entity_catalog.name: Ferdowsi
entity_catalog.role: Author of the Shahnameh; cited as preserver of Iran's civilizational memory and myths of liberty

entity_catalog.name: Rostam
entity_catalog.role: Hero of Shahnameh; analyzed in context of the impossibility of a state official remaining national

entity_catalog.name: Esfandiyar
entity_catalog.role: Hero of Shahnameh; conflict with Rostam analyzed as state vs. civilization tension

entity_catalog.name: Bahram Gur
entity_catalog.role: Sassanid king; cited for Charter of Limited Kingship: 'a king who calls the people kings over their own possessions'

entity_catalog.name: Zahhak
entity_catalog.role: Mythological Iranian tyrant; analyzed as the pattern of state, power, and deception; Satan feeds on his shoulders

entity_catalog.name: Iblis (Satan)
entity_catalog.role: Religious figure; analyzed as mechanism of servitude to other-than-God; analogized to the State

entity_catalog.name: Iran
entity_catalog.role: Central civilizational subject; defined as land of proprietors; two-sided wound: distortion of religion and theft of history

entity_catalog.name: Islamic Republic of Iran
entity_catalog.role: Current Iranian state; analyzed as product of historical roots; its 1979 revolution analyzed via time preference

entity_catalog.name: Shi'ism
entity_catalog.role: Branch of Islam; analyzed as inherently liberty-oriented if purified; popular continuity in Iran explained

entity_catalog.name: Islam
entity_catalog.role: Primary religion analyzed; interpreted as formal system of liberty; entry into Iran analyzed as transformative theory

entity_catalog.name: Christianity
entity_catalog.role: Compared to Islam; Trinity analyzed as source of dialectical mysticism; Church cited as historical instrument of state-religion conflation

entity_catalog.name: Zoroastrianism
entity_catalog.role: Ancient Iranian religion; Zurvanism and Manichaeism cited as lineage of mysticism

entity_catalog.name: Austrian School of Economics
entity_catalog.role: Economic tradition (Mises, Hayek, Rothbard); cited approvingly but critiqued as incomplete without theological grounding

entity_catalog.name: Libertarianism
entity_catalog.role: Political philosophy; cited approvingly but described as incomplete system without religious foundation

entity_catalog.name: Socialism / Communism
entity_catalog.role: Analyzed as envy armed with greed; anti-human; anti-liberty; rooted in mysticism and statism

entity_catalog.name: New Atheism
entity_catalog.role: Represented by Ayaan Hirsi Ali; analyzed as incapable of winning civilizational war

entity_catalog.name: Nazism / Fascism
entity_catalog.role: Cited as outcome of Hegelian-dialectical lineage; product of anti-liberty mysticism

entity_catalog.name: Umayyad Caliphate
entity_catalog.role: Historical Islamic state; analyzed as usurpation of religion in favor of power; oppressor of Iranians

entity_catalog.name: Abbasid Caliphate
entity_catalog.role: Historical Islamic state; oppression of Iranians cited as deepening bond with Ahl al-Bayt

entity_catalog.name: Sassanid Empire
entity_catalog.role: Pre-Islamic Iranian empire; its taxation cited; Bahram Gur's charter analyzed; context for Islam's appeal in Iran

entity_catalog.name: Suffolk Bank
entity_catalog.role: Historical American bank; cited as witness to viability of free banking and private clearing

entity_catalog.name: Central Bank
entity_catalog.role: Described as 'earthly god'; analyzed as monopolistic state tool contradicting liberty; inflation generator

entity_catalog.name: Gödel's Incompleteness Theorems
entity_catalog.role: Mathematical/logical theorems; applied to argue religion must be an open, consistent system; used to critique Hilbert's formalism

entity_catalog.name: Praxeology
entity_catalog.role: Austrian economic methodology (Mises); cited as basis for praxeological inference of the theory of liberty

entity_catalog.name: Game Theory
entity_catalog.role: Applied to unsupervised store theft problem; used to model deterrence and last-round effects

entity_catalog.name: Artificial Intelligence (AI)
entity_catalog.role: Central to the book's challenge: consistent formal ethical system must be programmable; AI as test of religious consistency

entity_catalog.name: AI Alignment
entity_catalog.role: Problem of aligning AI with human values; the theory of liberty claims to be the only existing solution

entity_catalog.name: Formal Axiomatic System
entity_catalog.role: Core methodological framework; religion must be representable as finite, non-contradictory axiomatic system

entity_catalog.name: Leviathan (Hobbes)
entity_catalog.role: Hobbes's political philosophy text; analyzed critically; Iran cited as historically independent of its model

entity_catalog.name: The Narrow Corridor (Acemoglu & Robinson)
entity_catalog.role: Analyzed and critiqued as 'another deception against liberty'

entity_catalog.name: Shahnameh (Ferdowsi)
entity_catalog.role: Epic of Iranian kings; analyzed as civilizational memory of liberty; myths decoded through theory of liberty

entity_catalog.name: The Qur'an
entity_catalog.role: Primary Islamic text; 'string of beads' metaphor applied; its challenge accepted as test of consistency

entity_catalog.name: Heraclitus
entity_catalog.role: Ancient Greek philosopher; cited in genealogy of political mysticism leading to Hegel

entity_catalog.name: Plato
entity_catalog.role: Ancient Greek philosopher; Platonism applied to Gödel's objectivism; Peikoff genealogy from Plato to Auschwitz

entity_catalog.name: Kant, Immanuel
entity_catalog.role: German philosopher; cited in Peikoff's genealogy; subject-centered reason criticized

entity_catalog.name: Zurvanism
entity_catalog.role: Ancient Iranian religious movement; cited as lineage of mysticism and the silencing of reason

entity_catalog.name: Manichaeism
entity_catalog.role: Gnostic religion; cited as lineage of dualism and mysticism in Iranian tradition

entity_catalog.name: Kurdish Koolbars
entity_catalog.role: Cross-border porters in Kurdistan; cited approvingly as legitimate market actors resisting state-imposed tariffs

entity_catalog.name: Mudarabah
entity_catalog.role: Islamic profit-sharing contract; cited as natural financial form under free banking

entity_catalog.name: Taghut
entity_catalog.role: Quranic term for illegitimate ruling power/idol; applied to any state that exceeds its bounds

entity_catalog.name: Ahl al-Bayt
entity_catalog.role: Prophet's household; Imams; eschatological reports cited as derived from axiomatic-inferential logic

entity_catalog.name: Wilayah (Guardianship)
entity_catalog.role: Islamic concept; distinguished from caliphate/state power; Ghadir as transfer of wilayah, not state

entity_catalog.name: Imamate
entity_catalog.role: Shi'i doctrine of succession; analyzed as preservation of religion against state capture

entity_catalog.name: Ghadir Khumm
entity_catalog.role: Event where Prophet designated Ali; reinterpreted as transfer of guardianship (wilayah), not political state

entity_catalog.name: 1979 Iranian Revolution
entity_catalog.role: Analyzed via time preference; 'Neither Shah nor Sheikh' position; religious state capture analyzed

entity_catalog.name: 22 September 2022
entity_catalog.role: Author's personal crisis: forced leap from window to preserve free-thought path; referenced in prefatory letter

entity_catalog.name: January 2026 Iran Struggle
entity_catalog.role: Cited as 'tragic struggle for Liberty, paid for in blood'; contemporary context for the book

entity_catalog.name: Coup of 19 August 1953 (Mossadegh Coup)
entity_catalog.role: Described as 'distortion of 19 August 1953 and the theft of history'; geopolitical analysis applied

entity_catalog.name: Learned Helplessness
entity_catalog.role: Psychological mechanism of domination; cited as tool of state control; overcome through hope and faith

entity_catalog.name: Jizya
entity_catalog.role: Islamic tax on non-Muslims; analyzed in comparison to Sassanid taxation as context for Islam's appeal in Iran

entity_catalog.name: Nazri (Votive offering)
entity_catalog.role: Grassroots voluntary charitable giving; contrasted with state-administered welfare

entity_catalog.name: Savak / State Security
entity_catalog.role: Implicit in context of author's arrest and judicial ambiguity; instruments of state control

entity_catalog.name: United States
entity_catalog.role: Referenced as location of AI companies; Reagan era cited; foreign policy implications discussed

entity_catalog.name: Europe / Western Civilization
entity_catalog.role: Analyzed critically: Western problem is intervention, not liberty; Renaissance-to-socialism path warned against

entity_catalog.name: Time Preference
entity_catalog.role: Austrian concept; cited in analysis of 1979 Revolution and land/civilization relationship

entity_catalog.name: Dopamine
entity_catalog.role: Neurological mechanism; cited in context of inflation and moral decline

entity_catalog.name: Entropy
entity_catalog.role: Thermodynamic concept; applied to model life, production, consumption, and defense of liberalism

entity_catalog.name: Reductio ad Absurdum
entity_catalog.role: Method of argument; used in critique of godless capitalism and resource crisis

entity_catalog.name: Amir al-Mu'minin Ali (AS)
entity_catalog.role: Same as Ali ibn Abi Talib; formal title used in caliphate context

entity_catalog.name: Abu Sufyan
entity_catalog.role: Meccan opponent of Islam; cited as pattern of confiscating religion for state power

entity_catalog.name: Imam Reza (AS)
entity_catalog.role: Eighth Imam; book's completion noted on his birth anniversary in Mashhad

entity_catalog.name: Mashhad
entity_catalog.role: Iranian city; location where book was completed

entity_catalog.name: Affirmative Action
entity_catalog.role: American policy; analyzed as institutionalized envy; violates equality of dignity

entity_catalog.name: Hilbert, David
entity_catalog.role: Mathematician; his formalist program cited as limited by 'anti-Platonist prejudice'; incompleteness theorems refute it
